original_hypothesis.title: Governance Bypass: Defeated and Expired Proposals Can Be Executed
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol execute() functions allow execution of proposals in Defeated, Expired, and Succeeded states. An attacker can create a malicious governance proposal containing arbitrary external calls, have it be voted down by the community, and still execute the malicious payload by calling execute().
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The require statement explicitly allows Defeated and Expired states: `require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired)`. This completely nullifies the governance voting mechanism and allows any proposer to execute arbitrary actions through the governor contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require allows ProposalState.Defeated and ProposalState.Expired in addition to Succeeded

preconditions: Attacker has enough voting power to meet proposalThreshold (default 0, so any holder)
Governor contract has been deployed and holds assets or controls privileged operations
Voting period completes (proposal reaches Defeated or Expired state)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: execute() requires status == Succeeded || Defeated || Expired instead of just Succeeded
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit proposal with arbitrary calldata targeting governor-controlled assets, let it be defeated, call execute()
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All assets controlled by governance are at risk - arbitrary contract calls are executed via _execute loop

economic_questions: What assets does the governor control or can relay to?
What is the proposalThreshold - can any token holder submit proposals?
Are there timelocks or additional guards on execution?
validation_requirements: Verify both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol execute() have the same bug
Confirm state() function returns Defeated/Expired correctly
Test end-to-end: propose, defeat, execute malicious payload

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for propose() + execute() calls (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to total value controlled by governance
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite ROI if governance controls significant assets

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after voting period ends

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users and governance participants
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets accessible via governor execute/relay functions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify state machine transitions for Defeated proposals

validation_result.mechanism: Governance bypass via execute() accepting Defeated/Expired proposals
validation_result.impact_summary: Both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol execute() functions accept proposals in Defeated and Expired states, allowing any proposer to execute arbitrary external calls through governance regardless of voting outcome.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The execute() function in both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol contains a require statement that explicitly allows execution of proposals in ProposalState.Defeated and ProposalState.Expired states, in addition to ProposalState.Succeeded. In standard OpenZeppelin Governor, only Succeeded (or Queued with timelock) proposals should be executable. This defect completely nullifies the governance voting mechanism. An attacker can submit a proposal with arbitrary targets and calldatas, have the community vote it down, and still call execute() to run the malicious payload. The proposalThreshold defaults to 0, meaning anyone with any voting power can propose. The _execute() internal function loops through targets making arbitrary external calls with provided values and calldatas, so any action the governor contract is authorized to perform can be triggered by a defeated proposal.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is unambiguously present in both contracts. Governor.sol lines ~257-266 and L2Governor.sol lines ~257-266 contain identical require statements: `require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired, ...)`. The state() function in both contracts correctly classifies proposals into Succeeded, Defeated, and Expired states based on quorum and vote counts, but execute() treats all three as valid for execution. The _execute() function then performs arbitrary calls: `targets[i].call{value: values[i]}(calldatas[i])`.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The require statement allows ProposalState.Defeated and ProposalState.Expired in addition to ProposalState.Succeeded. Should only allow ProposalState.Succeeded.
Identical defect in L2Governor.sol - the require statement allows execution of Defeated and Expired proposals.
This function performs arbitrary external calls to attacker-specified targets with attacker-specified calldata, making the impact of the execute() bug critical.
The state() function correctly classifies proposals, but execute() then accepts all three terminal states (Succeeded, Defeated, Expired) as valid for execution.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls propose() with malicious targets/calldatas (proposalThreshold is 0)
2. Voting period passes; community votes Against, making state() return Defeated
3. Alternatively, no quorum is reached, making state() return Expired
4. Attacker calls execute(targets, values, calldatas, epochTimeHash)
5. hashProposal computes proposalId from provided parameters
7. require passes because it accepts Defeated and Expired
9. _execute() loops through targets making arbitrary external calls
10. Malicious payload executes with governor contract's authority
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: execute() at Governor.sol:257 -> hashProposal() computes ID -> state() returns Defeated/Expired -> require at line ~266 PASSES because it includes Defeated and Expired -> _execute() at Governor.sol:276 loops through targets[i].call{value: values[i]}(calldatas[i]) executing arbitrary code. The same path exists in L2Governor.sol with identical logic.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: V1/deployGovernor.js shows simple deployment: `data = await ethers.getContractFactory("Governor"); governor = await data.deploy();`. No additional configuration that would mitigate this bug.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is fully validated: (1) proposalThreshold() returns 0, so any token holder can propose. (2) Attacker calls propose() with malicious targets (e.g., calling relay() to drain governor-held assets, or updateQuorumNumerator to set quorum to 0). (3) Community votes the proposal down - state() returns Defeated. (4) Attacker calls execute() with the same parameters - the require check passes because Defeated is explicitly allowed. (5) _execute() makes the malicious external calls. The error message 'Governor: proposal not successful or defeated' even suggests the developer intended to handle both outcomes but incorrectly allowed execution for all of them instead of just Succeeded.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL - This completely undermines the governance mechanism. Any proposal that reaches a terminal state (which all proposals eventually do) can be executed, regardless of whether the community voted for or against it. Assets held by the governor, permissions controlled by onlyGovernance functions (updateQuorumNumerator, relay), and any protocol operations that go through governance are all at risk. The relay() function allows the governor to make arbitrary calls, meaning all assets accessible to the governor can be stolen.
recommended_mitigations: Change the require in execute() to only allow ProposalState.Succeeded: `require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded, "Governor: proposal not successful");`
Apply this fix to both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol
Add a proposalThreshold > 0 to prevent spam proposals (defense in depth)
Consider adding a timelock between successful vote and execution for additional safety
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Governor contract with a mock IBlackHoleVotes token. 2. Give attacker address 1 voting power. 3. Attacker calls propose() with targets=[victimContract] and calldatas=[maliciousCalldata]. 4. Wait for voting period to start. 5. Have other users vote Against with majority. 6. Wait for voting period to end. 7. Verify state() returns Defeated. 8. Attacker calls execute() with same parameters. 9. Verify the malicious calldata was executed on the victim contract.
uncertainties: The concrete Governor.sol contract appears to be deployed standalone (V1/deployGovernor.js) but it's unclear which concrete implementation inherits from the abstract L2Governor - the actual deployed Governor may combine Governor.sol's abstract classes differently
It is unclear what assets or privileges the deployed governor contract actually controls in production
additional_testing_needed: Verify which concrete contract is deployed in production and whether it inherits from Governor.sol or L2Governor.sol
Enumerate all assets and permissions controlled by the deployed governor contract
Check if there's an external timelock or multisig that acts as executor (though this wouldn't prevent the bug, just change the attack path)
